Cannabis Chemistry Calculator
Save Preset
Load Preset
CCC
Dashboard
Quick Batch
Decarb
Infusion
Dose
Methods
Advanced Tools
Knowledge
Journal
First-Timer Guide

Your foolproof walkthrough from flower to edible

I know what I am doing — take me to the full app
What you need
Prepare your material
Decarb
Infuse
Figure out your dose
Make your edibles

Do not worry if you do not have everything on this list. Most items have easy substitutions, and the one thing you cannot mess up is the temperature.

Cannabis flower

Any amount works. Quality matters more than quantity.

Glass baking dish

A ceramic casserole dish or even a pie plate works fine.

Aluminum foil

An oven-safe lid or tight layer of parchment plus foil.

Butter or coconut oil

Ghee or any oil with some fat content works. Avoid watery oils.

An oven

A toaster oven with a temperature dial works too.

A stove or slow cooker

A double boiler or even a very low oven works in a pinch.

A strainer or cheesecloth

A clean kitchen towel, fine mesh sieve, or nut-milk bag.

Something to bake

Brownie mix, cookie dough, cake mix, gummies, whatever you like.

The one thing you cannot mess up

Keep the oven at 235°F (113°C) and do not open the door during the first hour. Opening the door drops the temperature and lets out the good stuff.

Next

screenshots.name: tab-knowledge
screenshots.text:
Cannabis Chemistry Calculator
Save Preset
Load Preset
CCC
Dashboard
Quick Batch
Decarb
Infusion
Dose
Methods
Advanced Tools
Knowledge
Journal
Choose Start
First-Timer Guide
Knowledge
Conversion Pathway

When you heat cannabis, a chemical chain reaction begins. First, the main inactive compound (THCA) converts into the active compound (THC) through a process called decarboxylation. This happens because heat removes a small carbon dioxide (CO₂) group from each THCA molecule, turning it into THC.[Wang et al. 2016][Tahir et al. 2021]

But heat is not selective. If you apply too much heat for too long, THC itself starts to break down into CBN, a much less potent compound that tends to cause drowsiness. That is why the same temperature that activates your material can also degrade it if you are not careful.[Jaidee et al. 2022][Garcia-Valverde et al. 2022]

How Decarboxylation Works
THCA
CO₂
THC

THCA loses its carboxyl group at ~105°C. The result is psychoactive THC.

12%
1x
The 0.877 Factor

You will notice the calculator multiplies your THCA percentage by 0.877 before turning it into milligrams. That number is not arbitrary -- it is the molecular weight ratio between THC and THCA.[Filer 2022]

THCA weighs about 358.47 g per mole. THC weighs about 314.45 g per mole. The difference (44.02 g) is the exact weight of the CO₂ group that gets removed during decarboxylation. Divide THC by THCA and you get 314.45 ÷ 358.47 ≈ 0.877. That is why 1 gram of pure THCA never yields 1 gram of THC -- you always lose that CO₂ fragment in the process.[Moreno et al. 2020]

CBDA to CBD Pathway

Cannabidiolic acid (CBDA) decarboxylates into cannabidiol (CBD) through the same mechanism as THCA → THC. CBDA and THCA are constitutional isomers: same atoms (C₂₂H₃₀O₄), same weight (~358.47 g/mol), different arrangement. Same structure means same 0.877 factor applies.[Moreno et al. 2020]

Despite being the same molecule reshuffled, CBDA decarboxylates faster than THCA. Its activation energy barrier is a touch lower, so CBD-rich material can hit good conversion at slightly cooler temperatures or shorter times than THC-dominant strains. The calculator still applies the same 0.877 ratio and efficiency ranges to both pathways — the molecular-weight math is identical.[Moreno et al. 2020]

Sous Vide Temperature Ceiling

Sous vide decarboxylation uses a hot water bath to heat the material gently and evenly. But there is a hard ceiling: water boils at 100°C (212°F) at standard atmospheric pressure. That means you physically cannot heat a water bath past that point unless you use a pressure cooker, which the calculator does not cover.

This is actually a feature, not a bug. Most home cooks do not want their decarb to exceed 100°C because higher temperatures destroy delicate compounds while also pushing THC toward CBN. The 95°C and 85°C presets stay safely below that boiling ceiling while still providing plenty of heat for full decarboxylation.[Wang et al. 2016][Moreno et al. 2020]

Vacuum Sealing and Oxidation

Oxygen is the enemy of fresh flavor and potency. When cannabis is exposed to air during heating, oxygen molecules attack terpenes (the aromatic compounds) and also accelerate the conversion of THC into CBN.[Garcia-Valverde et al. 2022][Eyal et al. 2023]

Vacuum-sealed sous vide bags remove almost all the air around the material before heat is applied. The result is better terpene preservation and less CBN formation compared with open-air oven methods. You can see this reflected in the presets: oven-open methods carry a higher CBN risk label because they expose the material directly to oxygen.[Raz et al. 2022]

Material Preparation

Coarse, chunky material retains terpenes better than fine powder. This sounds counterintuitive -- more surface area should mean faster heat transfer -- but the key is what happens before and during heating.[Eyal et al. 2023]

Fine powder has more total surface area exposed to air. Volatile terpenes begin evaporating the moment you gri
